sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=21, col=19).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 46, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves green pixel right 2px within container (VERIFIED step 3)', 'LEFT': 'Moves green pixel left 2px within container (VERIFIED step 4)', 'UP': 'Moves green up within container if space exists (VERIFIED). No portal swap ever occurred - previous claim was wrong.', 'DOWN': 'Moves green down within container if space exists (VERIFIED)', 'MOUSE': 'Clicking on green pixel causes HUD timer bar to shrink by 1 pixel (VERIFIED step 42). Second click has no additional effect.'}
- Object taxonomy: {'green_pixel': 'Active 2x2 green (N) object, controlled by arrow keys', 'divider_blocks': 'White 2x2 blocks at col 32-33, vertical line', 'colored_pixels': '2x2 objects in left containers: yellow, blue, red, dark red, green', 'hud_timer': 'White bar at row 63, shrinks when MOUSE clicks green pixel'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'White horizontal bar at row 63, shrinks as timer/progress indicator. Decreases by 1 pixel per MOUSE click on green.', 'divider': 'Vertical line of white 2x2 blocks at col 32-33'}
Current level:
- World model: Two-panel layout. Left (charcoal bg) has gray containers with colored 2x2 pixels. Right (black bg) has white containers with red/blue shapes. Green pixel is the active object, moves within bottom container (rows 38-43, cols 8-13). MOUSE click on green shrinks the timer bar by 1. The timer bar started at ~44 pixels and is now ~43. This suggests the game is about clicking green a certain number of times, or clicking green after positioning it in specific locations.
- Goal model: The timer bar shrinks when clicking green. Goal may be to shrink it to zero, or to click green at specific positions to trigger color transfers to the right side. Need to explore whether clicking green at different positions or clicking other colored pixels has different effects.
- Recent findings: ['MOUSE on green at (42,12) caused board_changed=True (step 42)', 'Change was only in HUD timer bar: white bar at row 63 shrunk by 1 pixel', 'Second MOUSE click on green had no effect (step 43)', 'No arrow key movement has ever caused board_changed=True']
- Open questions: ['Does clicking green at different positions have different effects?', 'Does clicking other colored pixels (yellow, blue, red) do anything?', 'Is the goal to shrink the timer to zero, or to trigger color transfers?', 'Do I need to move green to different containers before clicking?']
- Plan: Try MOUSE on other colored pixels (yellow, blue, red) to see if they have different effects. Also try moving green to different positions and clicking there.
- Strategy log: ['Abandoned portal swap theory: no action ever caused board_changed until MOUSE on green (step 42)', 'Movement actions (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT) never cause board changes - only MOUSE does']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(20,24) on yellow pixel: no selection change (step 9)', 'UP at cols 8-9 rows 38-39: no change (step 16)', 'UP at cols 10-11 rows 38-39: no change (step 17)', 'RIGHT at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no movement (step 13)', 'UP at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no change (step 12)', 'UP at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no move (step 37)', 'RIGHT from cols 12-13 rows 42-43: no move', 'MOUSE on green second time: no additional effect (step 43)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 45 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking on the blue 4x4 object at (21, 19) caused board_changed=True and the timer went from 42 to 40 (shrunk by 2, not 1!). This is different from green and yellow which only shrunk by 1 each.
Let me analyze the pattern:
- Green (2x2, 4 pixels): timer shrunk by 1
- Yellow (2x2, 4 pixels): timer shrunk by 1
- Blue (4x4, 16 pixels): timer shrunk by 2
So the timer shrink amount might be related to the size of the object! Let me check:
- 4 pixels -> 1 shrink
- 16 pixels -> 2 shrink
Maybe it's sqrt(pixels)? sqrt(4) = 2, sqrt(16) = 4. No, that doesn't match.
Or maybe it's log2(pixels)? log2(4) = 2, log2(16) = 4. No.
Or maybe it's pixels/4? 4/4 = 1, 16/4 = 4. No, blue only shrunk by 2.
Or maybe it's related to the side length? 2x2 -> 1, 4x4 -> 2. So side_length/2?
Wait, let me reconsider. The timer went from 42 to 40, which is a decrease of 2. But maybe there's something else going on. Let me check if the blue object itself changed.
Actually, I should also check if clicking on the right-side objects (red T-shape, blue L-shape) does anything.
But first, let me try clicking on the red object (24 pixels, 4x6 rectangle) to see how much the timer shrinks.
Also, I notice there are some single-pixel blue objects (b: pixels=1) at various positions. These might be decorative or part of the game mechanics.
Let me also think about the goal. The timer started at ~44 and is now at 40. If the goal is to reduce it to 0, I'd need to click on many more objects. But there are only a few clickable objects on the left side.
Wait, let me reconsider the game structure:
- Left side: gray containers with colored objects
- Right side: white containers with colored shapes
- The right side has a red T-shape (47 pixels) and a blue L-shape (47 pixels)
Maybe the game is about matching colors between left and right? Or maybe clicking on left objects "fills" the right shapes?
Let me try clicking on the red object on the left (24 pixels) to see what happens.
Actually, let me also check if the blue object changed after clicking it. Maybe it disappeared or changed color.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blue click shrunk timer by 2 (42->40). Let me check if blue object changed, and try clicking other objects.

# Check current state of all colored objects
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Current colored objects (non-bg, non-divider):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['W', 'G', 'c', 'B']:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}...")

# The blue 4x4 was at [[20, 18], [20, 21], [23, 21], [23, 18]] with 16 pixels
# Check if it still exists
blue_exists = False
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 16:
        blue_exists = True
        print(f"\nBlue 4x4 still exists: {n['boundary']}")
        break
if not blue_exists:
    print("\nBlue 4x4 is GONE!")

# Try clicking on the red object (24 pixels, boundary [[30, 12], ...])
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 14}])
print(f"\nAfter red click: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and 30 < n['pixels'] < 100:
        print(f"Timer: {n['pixels']}")
        break
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves green right 2px", "LEFT": "Moves green left 2px", "UP": "Moves green up if space", "DOWN": "Moves green down if space", "MOUSE": "Clicking colored objects shrinks timer. Amount depends on object size: 2x2->1, 4x4->2"}, "object_taxonomy": {"green_pixel": "Active 2x2 green object", "divider_blocks": "White 2x2 at col 32-33", "colored_pixels": "Various colored objects in left containers", "right_shapes": "Red T-shape and blue L-shape on right"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "White bar row 63, timer"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Timer shrinks by clicking colored objects. Shrink amount may relate to object size.", "goal_model": "Reduce timer to zero or match colors to right shapes", "recent_findings": ["Green (2x2): timer -1", "Yellow (2x2): timer -1", "Blue (4x4): timer -2"], "open_questions": ["Does timer shrink relate to object size?", "What happens to clicked objects?", "Do right-side shapes matter?"], "current_plan": "Click remaining left objects to see pattern", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Second click on same pixel: no effect", "Click on red (wrong coords): no effect"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
